New Panama Canal Treaty : environmental impact statement.

The proposed action involves a new treaty between the United States and Panama regarding the operation and defense of the Panama Canal. This treaty aims to replace the outdated 1903 agreement, which has been a source of tension between the two countries. The new treaty seeks to ensure the canal remains open and neutral, while also addressing environmental concerns and job losses for U.S. and Panamanian employees. The treaty's implementation could lead to increased rail traffic in the U.S. due to potential toll hikes. The community and environment could be significantly impacted, especially if environmental protections are not effectively enforced.

The New Panama Canal Treaty was proposed to update the longstanding 1903 agreement between the U.S. and Panama, which had been a point of contention for decades. There was significant public pushback from various groups concerned about sovereignty, environmental impacts, and job security for workers. Ultimately, the treaty was signed in 1977 and led to the gradual transfer of control of the canal to Panama, which was completed in 1999.

"We are seeking to prepare the impact statement in time to be relevant to Senate consideration of the proposed treaty. This injects an element of great urgency into our process." — Ms. Patsy T. Mink, Assistant Secretary of State for Oceans and International Environmental and Scientific Affairs
